content.text: A pull request (PR) is the central collaboration artifact in modern software development — a formal proposal to merge one branch into another, bundling a code diff, a description, automated CI checks, and a structured review conversation into a single trackable unit. Mastering the PR workflow is as essential for AI/ML engineers shipping agent pipelines as it is for web developers shipping features.

A pull request is a hosted diff between two Git branches with an attached discussion thread. On GitHub, GitLab, and Bitbucket the PR lives as a first-class object in the remote repository. It shows every commit on the feature branch that has not yet reached the target branch (usually

), the cumulative line-level diff, a side-by-side file view, inline comment threads anchored to specific lines, and a checklist of required status checks and approvals that must pass before the branch can be merged.

The name comes from the action of asking the maintainer to "pull" your commits into their branch. In the GitHub model, the author opens a PR from their fork or feature branch; in the GitLab model the equivalent is called a Merge Request (MR), but the mechanics are identical. The PR workflow replaced the older practice of emailing patches as
files and is now the de-facto standard for collaborative software development whether the team is two people or two thousand.

For AI/ML projects specifically, PRs serve an additional purpose: they capture the reasoning behind architectural decisions — why a particular prompt template was chosen, why a retrieval chunk size was changed, why an eval threshold was updated — providing durable institutional memory that lives alongside the code rather than in a separate wiki or Slack channel.

Without a pull request process, changes go directly to the main branch. This creates several painful problems: regressions are discovered in production rather than before merge; it is difficult to attribute why a particular change was made months later; and there is no natural point to run automated checks, solicit review, or catch errors before they become customer-visible. A single mistyped prompt in a production agent can produce wrong answers at scale — a PR with required eval checks catches this before deployment.

PRs also enforce a social contract on teams. The author is asked to explain what they changed and why, which surfaces implicit assumptions and prompts cleaner thinking. Reviewers are asked to engage critically before approving. This friction is deliberate — it filters out changes that are not ready while creating an auditable record of who reviewed and approved every change that did ship.

In regulated industries (finance, healthcare, legal AI tools), the PR audit trail is often a compliance requirement. Every approval, every comment, and the exact diff that was merged are preserved immutably in the Git history and the PR metadata, making it straightforward to reconstruct exactly what code was running at any point in time.

— reviewers see code they did not write, spreading understanding of architecture decisions across the team. This reduces bus-factor risk on critical subsystems like agent orchestration loops or retrieval pipelines.
— a second pair of eyes catches logic errors, missing edge cases, and insecure patterns before they reach production. Code review finds defects at a fraction of the cost of fixing them post-release.
— required status checks (unit tests, linting, type checking, eval suites) block merge until all pass, ensuring no branch bypasses quality gates.

— review threads are persistent, searchable, and async-friendly. A reviewer in a different timezone can leave comments that the author addresses when they come online, without scheduling a synchronous meeting.

— each PR corresponds to a discrete unit of work. When a regression is traced to a particular merge, the PR provides instant context: what changed, why, who approved, and which tests were green at merge time.
— opening a PR as a draft signals work-in-progress and invites early architectural feedback before the implementation is complete, avoiding wasted effort going in the wrong direction.

The PR process adds latency to the development cycle. A feature that could be pushed directly to main in seconds instead waits for CI to run (minutes to hours for full eval suites) and for at least one reviewer to respond (hours to days depending on team culture). For very small teams doing rapid experimentation, this overhead can slow iteration velocity.

— if a small team has one senior engineer who must approve everything, PR queues back up and block progress. Required reviewers must be carefully chosen or set as a minimum count rather than specific individuals.
— under deadline pressure, reviewers may approve without carefully reading the diff, creating false security. The process is only as valuable as the quality of review.

— long-lived feature branches that diverge significantly from main create painful merge conflicts. The larger the PR, the harder it becomes to review and the more likely it conflicts with concurrent work.
— a 2,000-line PR is genuinely hard to review well. Without adequate context in the description, reviewers cannot efficiently evaluate whether the approach is correct.
— without a written review guide, review quality varies dramatically between team members, creating inconsistent gatekeeping.

The typical PR lifecycle begins when a developer pushes a feature branch to the remote repository. GitHub automatically detects the new branch and suggests opening a PR. The author navigates to the repository UI, clicks "New pull request", selects the base branch (usually

) and the compare branch (the feature branch), writes a title and description, assigns reviewers, and optionally adds labels and linked issues. Submitting the form creates the PR object and triggers any configured CI workflows.

Once open, the PR diff view shows every changed file. Reviewers click any line in the diff to start a comment thread anchored to that specific line. Inline comments can request changes, ask questions, or suggest alternative code. GitHub's "Suggest a change" feature lets reviewers propose exact replacement code that the author can accept with one click. Unresolved threads block the PR from being merged if the repository has that protection enabled.
When a reviewer is satisfied, they submit a formal review with one of three states:
(blocking — must be resolved before merge). Once the required number of approvals is reached and all required CI checks are green, the merge button becomes active.
At merge time, three strategies are available. A
preserves the full branch history and creates a merge commit that joins the two lines.
collapses all branch commits into a single commit on main, keeping main history clean at the cost of losing intermediate commit messages.
replays the branch commits onto the tip of main without a merge commit, producing a linear history. Each strategy has implications for how
The diagram below shows the full PR lifecycle from feature branch creation through CI, review, merge, and deployment, as well as the key components that make up a PR object.

attrs.alt: Pull Request Lifecycle and Anatomy diagram

content.text: The PR workflow is appropriate for virtually every non-trivial change to a shared codebase. The main question is not whether to use PRs but how to scope them. Use a PR for each of the following:
Any change to a production agent pipeline, prompt template, or system prompt — even one-line edits, since prompt changes can silently alter model behavior across thousands of invocations.
New features, bug fixes, dependency upgrades, configuration changes, and infrastructure-as-code changes.
Eval suite additions or modifications — since eval code shapes the quality bar the team optimizes for, peer review on eval changes is especially important.
Changes to CI/CD pipeline definitions, deployment scripts, and secrets configuration — these changes have broad blast radius and warrant extra scrutiny.

PRs are less appropriate in a few situations. On a single-developer personal project with no collaborators, the overhead of reviewing your own work may not justify the ceremony — though the CI gating benefits still apply. For purely experimental scratch branches that will never be merged, opening a full PR is unnecessary. And some very rapid iteration loops (tuning a prompt locally against a test set) are better done on a local branch before opening a PR with the final result.
Setting up a robust PR workflow on GitHub involves configuring branch protection, writing a PR template, and adding CI checks. The following walkthrough covers each step.
Navigate to repository Settings → Branches → Add branch protection rule. Set the branch name pattern to

and enable: require a pull request before merging, require at least 1 approval, dismiss stale pull request approvals when new commits are pushed, require status checks to pass before merging (add your CI workflow names here), and require branches to be up to date before merging. Optionally restrict who can push to the branch directly.
in the repository root. This file pre-fills the PR description form every time a new PR is opened, reminding authors what information to provide.

## Summary

Briefly describe what this PR does and why.

## Type of change

- [ ] Bug fix (non-breaking change)
- [ ] New feature
- [ ] Breaking change
- [ ] Prompt / system prompt change
- [ ] Evaluation suite change
- [ ] Infrastructure / CI change

## Testing

Describe how you tested this change:
- Unit tests added/updated: yes/no
- Eval suite run: yes/no — results:
- Manual testing: describe steps

## Related issues

Closes #

## Checklist

- [ ] Self-reviewed the diff
- [ ] Code follows project conventions
- [ ] Tests pass locally
- [ ] Documentation updated if needed

# .github/workflows/ci.yml
name: CI

on:
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]
  push:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Set up Python
        uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: '3.11'
          cache: 'pip'

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: pip install -r requirements.txt

      - name: Lint
        run: |
          pip install ruff
          ruff check .

      - name: Type check
        run: |
          pip install mypy
          mypy src/

      - name: Run tests
        run: pytest tests/ -v --tb=short

      - name: Run evals (smoke)
        # Only run when prompt-related files change
        if: |
          contains(github.event.commits[0].modified, 'src/prompts/')
        run: python scripts/run_evals.py --suite smoke --fail-below 0.85
        env:
          OPENAI_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.OPENAI_API_KEY }}
Step 4 — Open a PR via the CLI

# Create and push a feature branch
git checkout -b feat/add-tool-retry-logic

# ... make changes ...
git add src/tools/retry.py tests/test_retry.py
git commit -m "feat: add exponential backoff retry for tool calls"
git push -u origin feat/add-tool-retry-logic

# Open PR with GitHub CLI (https://cli.github.com)
gh pr create \
  --title "feat: add exponential backoff retry for tool calls" \
  --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
## Summary
Adds configurable exponential backoff when tool calls return transient errors.
Without retry logic, a 503 from an external API fails the entire agent run.

## Testing
- Unit tests added: yes (tests/test_retry.py)
- Eval suite run: yes — pass rate 0.92 (was 0.89 before this change)
- Manual testing: ran against staging for 20 minutes

Closes #47
EOF
  )" \
  --reviewer alice,bob \
  --label "enhancement"

# Check PR status and CI results
gh pr status
gh pr checks

# When all checks pass and reviewer approves, merge with squash
gh pr merge --squash --delete-branch
Example 1 — Automating PR creation from a Python script

The following script uses the GitHub REST API to open a PR programmatically. This is useful for automated content pipelines (such as a script that generates new educational content and opens a PR for human review) or for release automation scripts.

"""Open a GitHub PR from Python using the REST API."""
import os
import json
from datetime import datetime
import urllib.request

GITHUB_TOKEN = os.environ["GITHUB_TOKEN"]
REPO = "my-org/my-agent-repo"
BASE_BRANCH = "main"


def open_pr(
    head_branch: str,
    title: str,
    body: str,
    draft: bool = False,
    reviewers: list[str] | None = None,
) -> dict:
    """Create a pull request and return the API response."""
    url = f"https://api.github.com/repos/{REPO}/pulls"
    payload = {
        "title": title,
        "head": head_branch,
        "base": BASE_BRANCH,
        "body": body,
        "draft": draft,
    }
    headers = {
        "Authorization": f"Bearer {GITHUB_TOKEN}",
        "Accept": "application/vnd.github+json",
        "X-GitHub-Api-Version": "2022-11-28",
        "Content-Type": "application/json",
    }
    req = urllib.request.Request(
        url,
        data=json.dumps(payload).encode(),
        headers=headers,
        method="POST",
    )
    with urllib.request.urlopen(req) as resp:
        pr_data = json.loads(resp.read())

    if reviewers:
        review_url = f"https://api.github.com/repos/{REPO}/pulls/{pr_data['number']}/requested_reviewers"
        review_req = urllib.request.Request(
            review_url,
            data=json.dumps({"reviewers": reviewers}).encode(),
            headers=headers,
            method="POST",
        )
        urllib.request.urlopen(review_req)

    return pr_data


if __name__ == "__main__":
    timestamp = datetime.utcnow().strftime("%Y%m%d-%H%M")
    branch = f"content/auto-generated-{timestamp}"

    pr = open_pr(
        head_branch=branch,
        title=f"content(agentic-ai): auto-generated subtopic {timestamp}",
        body=(
            "## Summary\n"
            "Automatically generated content for the next empty sub-subtopic.\n\n"
            "## Checklist\n"
            "- [ ] Review generated content for accuracy\n"
            "- [ ] Verify evals pass\n"
        ),
        draft=True,
        reviewers=["content-reviewer"],
    )
    print(f"Opened PR #{pr['number']}: {pr['html_url']}")
Example 2 — Posting automated inline review comments

"""Post an automated inline review comment on a PR diff.

Useful for bots that run eval checks and need to surface results
directly in the PR diff view rather than in a separate CI log.
"""
import os
import json
import urllib.request

GITHUB_TOKEN = os.environ["GITHUB_TOKEN"]
REPO = "my-org/my-agent-repo"


def post_review_comment(
    pr_number: int,
    commit_sha: str,
    path: str,
    line: int,
    body: str,
) -> dict:
    """Add an inline comment to a specific line in a PR diff."""
    url = f"https://api.github.com/repos/{REPO}/pulls/{pr_number}/comments"
    payload = {
        "body": body,
        "commit_id": commit_sha,
        "path": path,
        "line": line,
        "side": "RIGHT",
    }
    headers = {
        "Authorization": f"Bearer {GITHUB_TOKEN}",
        "Accept": "application/vnd.github+json",
        "X-GitHub-Api-Version": "2022-11-28",
        "Content-Type": "application/json",
    }
    req = urllib.request.Request(
        url,
        data=json.dumps(payload).encode(),
        headers=headers,
        method="POST",
    )
    with urllib.request.urlopen(req) as resp:
        return json.loads(resp.read())


def submit_review(
    pr_number: int,
    commit_sha: str,
    verdict: str,
    summary: str,
) -> dict:
    """Submit a pull request review.

    verdict: one of 'APPROVE', 'REQUEST_CHANGES', 'COMMENT'
    """
    url = f"https://api.github.com/repos/{REPO}/pulls/{pr_number}/reviews"
    payload = {
        "commit_id": commit_sha,
        "body": summary,
        "event": verdict,
    }
    headers = {
        "Authorization": f"Bearer {GITHUB_TOKEN}",
        "Accept": "application/vnd.github+json",
        "X-GitHub-Api-Version": "2022-11-28",
        "Content-Type": "application/json",
    }
    req = urllib.request.Request(
        url,
        data=json.dumps(payload).encode(),
        headers=headers,
        method="POST",
    )
    with urllib.request.urlopen(req) as resp:
        return json.loads(resp.read())


# Eval bot: post a comment if a prompt change drops eval score
if __name__ == "__main__":
    pr_number = 42
    commit_sha = "abc1234def5678"

    post_review_comment(
        pr_number=pr_number,
        commit_sha=commit_sha,
        path="src/prompts/system_prompt.txt",
        line=7,
        body=(
            ":robot: **Eval bot**: This change reduced the helpfulness score "
            "from 0.91 to 0.84 on the golden test set.\n\n"
            "[View eval dashboard](https://evals.example.com/run/pr-42)"
        ),
    )

    submit_review(
        pr_number=pr_number,
        commit_sha=commit_sha,
        verdict="REQUEST_CHANGES",
        summary="Eval score regression detected. See inline comment on system_prompt.txt.",
    )

Consider an AI team maintaining a customer-support agent that processes tens of thousands of requests per day. The team stores agent configuration — including system prompts, tool definitions, retry policies, and fallback logic — as code in a Git repository. Every change, no matter how minor, goes through a PR.
A typical prompt improvement PR follows this flow. An engineer opens a draft PR updating

to improve accuracy on refund-related queries. The PR description includes a before/after comparison of responses on five representative test cases. CI runs two jobs: the full unit test suite (30 seconds) and an eval job that runs 200 golden examples against both the old and new prompt, reporting pass rates. The PR is blocked from merging until both jobs are green and the eval job shows at least 90% pass rate on the core task with no regression on safety tests.

The ML engineer reviews the diff, runs a few ad-hoc queries against a staging deployment linked in the PR, and leaves three inline comments suggesting tone adjustments. The author pushes two more commits to address the comments. GitHub automatically re-requests review from the original reviewer (stale approval dismissal is enabled). The reviewer approves the second iteration. An automated merge bot checks that the "ready to merge" label is present and the branch is up-to-date with main, then squash-merges the PR and deletes the branch. The CD pipeline picks up the merge to main and deploys the new prompt to the staging environment within four minutes.

At this scale, the PR record becomes a searchable audit log. Searching PRs for "refund" shows the entire history of prompt iterations targeting refund queries, including the reviewer discussions that shaped each decision. When a customer escalation reveals an edge case, the team can immediately identify the PR that introduced the relevant prompt clause and understand exactly what tradeoff was made.
Massive PRs that span multiple unrelated features.

A PR adding a new tool and also refactoring the retry logic and also updating the prompt template is three PRs masquerading as one. Reviewers cannot evaluate them independently, and any single part being problematic blocks all three changes.
A PR titled "fix bug" with an empty body forces the reviewer to reconstruct intent from the diff alone. Always write at least two sentences explaining what changed and why.

A force-push rewrites history, making it impossible for reviewers to see what changed between the version they reviewed and the current one. Push new commits instead — the PR diff shows only the incremental changes.
Even when branch protection is not enforced, waiting for CI results is essential. A failing lint or test is a signal that should be investigated before merge.
Once a secret appears in a Git commit it is effectively compromised, even if removed in a subsequent commit — the history persists. Use pre-commit hooks with

A rubber-stamp approval undermines the entire purpose of code review and creates false assurance. If time pressure prevents real review, communicate that explicitly rather than clicking approve anyway.

Aim for PRs that can be reviewed in under 30 minutes — typically fewer than 400 lines of diff and a single coherent change. Small PRs get reviewed faster, land sooner, and have smaller blast radius when they contain bugs.
Describe the problem being solved, not just what the code does. Link to the issue or specification. If the implementation involves a non-obvious tradeoff, explain it explicitly.
Review your own diff before requesting review.
Opening the PR and reading the diff from the reviewer's perspective often catches debug print statements, leftover TODOs, and accidental white-space changes before anyone else sees them.
Draft PRs make the branch visible, trigger CI, and invite early architectural feedback without creating social pressure to approve immediately.
Even when not making the requested change, acknowledge it with an explanation. Silent dismissal of feedback loses institutional knowledge.
Stale branches accumulate quickly and create confusion about what is current. Enable automatic branch deletion after merge in GitHub repository settings.
Regularly rebase or merge main into long-lived feature branches to minimize the merge conflict surface. The "Update branch" button in GitHub makes this one click.

The PR process itself is lightweight — creating, reviewing, and merging PRs is bounded by human time rather than computational cost. The performance concern is CI turnaround: a workflow that takes 45 minutes to complete creates 45-minute feedback loops that slow development velocity significantly.

Strategies for fast CI on PR workflows include dependency caching (pip/poetry/npm caches reduce install time from minutes to seconds), parallelising test shards across multiple runner instances (GitHub Actions matrix strategy), and running expensive checks (full eval suites, integration tests) only when relevant files change using

filters. A tiered approach works well: fast unit tests and linting run on every commit (target: under 5 minutes), while longer eval suites run as required checks only for changes to prompt files or model configuration (target: under 20 minutes).

At large scale, teams with many simultaneous PRs can face merge queue contention — all open PRs want to merge to main, and each one must be rebased and re-tested after every preceding merge. GitHub's native Merge Queue feature solves this by serialising and batching merges, testing groups of PRs together to increase throughput while maintaining the invariant that main only ever contains tested combinations.
is the dominant platform for open-source and enterprise repositories. Its PR UI is mature, with code owners (
file automatically assigns reviewers), review assignment via teams, native merge queue, and GitHub Actions for CI.
offers an equivalent "Merge Request" workflow with a tightly integrated CI/CD system that many enterprises prefer for its self-hosted deployment options.
provides similar functionality and integrates tightly with Jira, making it popular in Atlassian-heavy organisations.
(GitHub CLI) is the official tool and covers the full PR lifecycle: create, view, review, merge, and close. Code quality tools that integrate with PRs include
(local hook runner that catches issues before push),
(prevents committing credentials), and Dependabot/Renovate (automated dependency update PRs).
Async inline comments, formal approval states
Excellent — every comment, approval, and CI result preserved
Thread-based, not anchored to code lines
Linux kernel and some open-source projects
No persistent record of review discussions

Branch protection is the primary security control for the PR process. Without it, any repository contributor can push directly to main, bypassing review and CI entirely. Always enable branch protection on main and any other long-lived branches that feed into deployments.
CI secrets (API keys for eval services, deployment credentials) must be stored in GitHub Actions secrets or equivalent, never in the repository. The

secret automatically injected into Actions workflows has scoped permissions — use it for PR operations rather than a personal access token with broad access. For third-party GitHub Actions, pin them to a specific commit SHA rather than a mutable tag to prevent supply-chain attacks.
to automatically detect and revoke accidentally committed API keys and tokens.
Enable Dependabot security alerts and version updates
— Dependabot opens automated PRs to bump vulnerable dependencies, making security patches follow the same review process as other changes.
Use CODEOWNERS to route sensitive files to the right reviewers.
to the ML team ensures prompt changes always get ML review.
This event grants secrets access to fork PRs but runs in the base branch context — never use it to check out untrusted fork code in a step that also has access to secrets.

Think of the main branch as a published magazine. Anyone can draft an article on their own notepad (feature branch), but before it appears in print it must be reviewed by an editor (code review), fact-checked against the style guide (CI linting and type checks), and approved by the editor-in-chief (required approvals). The draft article never goes directly to print — it always passes through the editorial process. Once published (merged), the article is permanent and forms part of the magazine's cumulative record.

The PR is the editorial submission form: it packages the draft, explains why the article should be published, documents the back-and-forth with the editor, and records the final sign-off. Long after publication, the submission form is still there, fully searchable, explaining exactly why that paragraph was worded the way it was.
A pull request is a structured proposal to merge a feature branch into main, bundling a diff, a description, CI status checks, and a review thread into a single auditable object.
Branch protection rules are the enforcement mechanism — without them the PR process is advisory only. Always protect main with required reviews and required status checks.
Small, focused PRs review faster, land sooner, and are easier to reason about when a regression is traced back to a specific merge.
The PR description is as important as the code diff — it provides context that the diff cannot express: the problem being solved, design alternatives considered, and known limitations.

For AI/ML projects, PR-gating eval results is especially important — prompt changes can silently degrade model behavior across thousands of invocations, and CI evals catch regressions before they reach production.
CI turnaround time is the main performance lever — fast feedback loops require caching, test parallelism, and path-based filtering to keep common PR checks under five minutes.
Security controls — secret scanning, pinned action SHAs, CODEOWNERS, and Dependabot — should be configured alongside the basic PR workflow, not added later as an afterthought.
